source_metadata.title_raw: Rick Kowalczyk - A-18-028 - March 8, 2018 - Half Moon Bay

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Rick Kowalczyk 
City Councilmember 
500 Main Street 
Half Moon Bay, CA 94019 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-028 
Dear Mr. Kowalczyk: 
March 8, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As Councilmember of the City of Half Moon Bay, do you have a disqualifying conflict of 
interest in tiling part in governmental decisions regarding the City's potential future development 
of a 21-acre parcel of real property located at 880 Stone Pine Road? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in any governmental decisions involving 
property at 880 Stone Pine Road. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
The City of Half Moon Bay and the Proposed Project: 
In 2004, the City acquired an approximately 21-acre parcel of real property located at 880 
Stone Pine Road, Half Moon Bay from the Peninsula Open Space Trust ("POST''). At the time, the 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-028 
Page No. 2 
City intended to construct a community park on the site. The $3. l million purchase was made 
possible by a loan from POST. 
For several reasons, including a lack of available funding, concerns from neighbors about 
noise, and the identification of an endangered species of frog on the site, the City decided to 
abandon its plans for a park. Instead, on July 23, 2009, the City adopted Resolution C-64-09. The 
resolution transferred title to 880 Stone Pine to POST in exchange for POST forgiving its loan to 
the City. Additionally, POST and the City entered into a license agreement permitting the City to 
operate its Corporations Maintenance Yard on the site. 
I 
On November 9, 2017, Walter T. Moore, the President of POST, sent a letter to the City 
regarding 880 Stone Pine. The letter states that POST does not intend to hold the property over the 
long term and offers to enter discussions with the City regarding either (a) selling the property to 
the City or (b) leasing the portion of the property used by the City in exchange for rental payments. 
The City Council discussed this letter on January 16, 2018. To date, the City has not made 
any decisions with respect to POST's letter and 880 Stone Pine. It has been reported in the local 
press that the City is considering various options for 880 Stone Pine, including (a) continuing to 
lease it as a Corporations Maintenance Yard, (b) purchasing it and developing it as a park, and (c) 
purchasing it and developing it for other uses, including affordable housing. 
Councilmember Rick Kowalczyk: 
You are a resident of Half Moon Bay and a City Councilmember. You own your home 
within a 500-foot radius of 880 Stone Pine. 
You recused yourself from previous discussions regarding 880 Stone Pine on January 16, 
2018 out of an abundance of caution. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, 
participates in making, or uses his or her position to· influence a governmental decision that has a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. 
Section 87103(b) indicates that an official has an interest in "[a]ny real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000." Your real property interest in your 
residence is the only interest implicated by the decisions. Accordingly, we must determine if the 
decisions will have a foreseeable and material effect. 
Foreseeability: 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decisions. (Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) 
Under the facts you have provided, your residence is not explicitly involved in the decisions. Thus, 


File No. A-18-028 
Page No. 3 
Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."2 
Materiality: 
The financial effects of 880 Stone Pine-related decisions on your real property interest are 
material if the decisions: 
• 
Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic 
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real 
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other 
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official 
has a financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l0).) 
• 
Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the 
property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property ... 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an 
official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on 
the official's property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1).) 
• 
Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property. 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(l2.) 
You indicated that it has been reported that the City is considering various options for 880 
Stone Pine, including (a) continuing to lease it as a Corporations Maintenance Yard, (b) purchasing 
it and developing it as a park, and (c) purchasing it and developing it for other uses, including 
affordable housing. It appears that decisions to purchase 880 Stone Pine are interrelated to its 
development potential. As such, it is reasonably foreseeable that purchase and development of a 
park or affordable housing could signific~tly increase traffic and noise levels, as well as parking. 
Moreover, considering your property is located within 500 feet of the parcel that is the subject of 
the decisions, the facts provided do not indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable 
measurable impact on the value of your residence. 
2 Regulation 1870 l(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the 
"reasonably foreseeable" determination. 


File No. A-18-028 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
~1.o~ 
By: 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division
